user_comment: This feed lets you know whenever a new note is published on this site. Copy the following URL -- https://ckunte.net/feed.json -- and add it to your feed reader.
title: C Kunte: weeknotes

items.title: git talk
items.content_html:
Despite the weather turning cold, it was a productive week; happy and exhausted. I also gave a talk, ‘version control for engineers’ to help with model management and quality control processes. Using Typst and git for commits, it was (for once) fun generating the viewgraphs, and practising the preach. My audience seemed heady, absorbing it all.

I wrote another snippet to build Typst document, Typst-py.sublime-build, right-off Sublime Text, this one particularly for my die-hard old MacBook Air (2011).

{
    "shell_cmd" : "python3 \\${HOME}/ctd.py -f \"$file_name\"",
    "selector" : "source.typ",
    "working_dir" : "$file_path"
}

items.title: In plain sight
items.content_html:
The sound of nostalgia has quietened on the group; to almost a lull. To ensure it holds and to avoid any stimulation, I have it on mute now.

I’ve begun to have fun writing and producing stuff with Typst (e.g. minutes, notes, progress logs, reports, and presentations). Just this week, I finished a slide pack, written in text entirely, which produces a really nice and professionally presentable deck. And yes, I can now track changes with git.

Another example is about conditional formatting. I like highlighting terms like tbc or tbd, so I can return to them later. This code below highlights and italicises any of these words: tbc, tbd. I can add it to a template without modifying the report’s main content; neat!

show regex("tb[c,d]"): set text(fill:red, style:"italic")

Not long ago, I stopped maintaining a public archive of my thoughts. After a threshold, weeknotes drop-off the only page they appear on (i.e. homepage), like the json feed I offer on this site. It’s not obvious, but appending #w to the URL will reveal the current.

items.title: Middle school nostalgia
items.content_html:
A week or so ago, someone from school whom I didn’t know asked sis for my number. Knowing me, she called first. I said yes. Another called to confirm, and after small talk, added me to a group with a hundred already on.

Despite my resistance to social media messaging, I was surprised to let myself be swept-off — sharing stuff, more than I thought I was capable of, with a bunch I barely knew back in the eighties and hardly recognise now.

Thirty-seven ignoramus years gone, and this group wants to have a “now or never” Class of 87 reunion in November. What’s more? A part of me wants to go.

items.title: Making room
items.content_html:
Out of options to get Typst on my die-hard MacBook Air (2011) via either Homebrew or MacPorts, I found this fantastic package called typst-py. To simplify further, I wrote an alias to complement my ctd.py script:

alias tc='_tc() { python3 /path-to/ctd.py -f $1 ;}; _tc'

With this, I typeset like so, and save a few key strokes:

$ tc note.typ
Compiling note.typ to note.pdf...done.

My younger one moves back to live with us from next week, and will commute to university from home. We’re looking forward to it very much. She seems to have misplaced her bike keys; will soon know if we need a hacksaw to bring it home.

Inspection is next week, and so this weekend is about housekeeping. Allied came back to collect empty boxes. The garage is neat again.

items.title: Shoebox
items.content_html:
Allied’s service was good. Their promise was 7-9 weeks; crating some part of their service. The container arrived at my doorstep in 8. Stuff intact, bar one. They reminded us to get transit bolts to protect the washer, which we did just in time.

We fixed the (a) dining table, (b) sideboard and bookcase shutters, and (c) shared work desk. We found the shoebox with useful bits and bobs, including my portable speaker.

Years in the making, numpy 2.0 landed this week. I got to test my scripts written over the years with the following; they’re still usable.

grep "numpy" *.py | ruff check --select NPY201
